message:
perf(mvcc): allocate_commit_seq SeqCst → AcqRel for counter consistency

`WriteCoordinator::allocate_commit_seq` was the only commit-seq
counter in the tree using `Ordering::SeqCst` for its `fetch_add`. The
two analogous counters use `AcqRel`:

  Connection::next_commit_seq (fsqlite-core/src/connection.rs:9468/9482)
    → fetch_add(1, AcqRel)
  CommitSequenceCombiner::next_commit_seq (commit_combiner.rs:322)
    → fetch_add(N, AcqRel)

`AcqRel` is the correct ordering for a monotonic counter under
release/acquire publication: the producer's Release pairs with any
reader's Acquire load (e.g. `SharedMemoryLayout::load_next_commit_seq`
in shm.rs:1083 already uses `Acquire`). `SeqCst` adds a strictly
unnecessary global total-order constraint with no use site that
relies on it.

The path is the WriteCoordinator's recovery / compatibility-mode
allocator (not the default MT publish path), so the win is small per
call. The change is taken for **consistency** with the rest of the
counter family and to remove the unique outlier — a future reader
auditing for ordering bugs no longer has to ask "why is this one
SeqCst?".

Behavior identical: `AcqRel` is strictly weaker than `SeqCst`, every
program correct under SeqCst is correct under AcqRel given the
release/acquire pair already in place. Nothing in the tree depends
on the SeqCst total-order property here.

Correctness: 13 write_coordinator::tests pass, including
`test_native_publish_requires_active_lease`,
`test_native_sequencer_tiny_marker`, and the WAL/restore lifecycle
tests.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
